perturbation.original_text: 4.1.3 the SP shall not disclose the confidential in formation of FP to any employee, consultant, professional adviser, contrac tor or sub- contractor or agent of the SP (collectively referre d to herein as "representative") of the SP or an affiliate of the SP, nor shall they be given access thereto by the SP 4.1.3.1 unless it is strictly necessary for the pur poses referred to in 2.1 above; and 4.1.3.2 the SP shall have procured that the represe ntative or affiliate to whom or to which such information is d isclosed or made available shall have agreed to be bound by all the terms of this agreement, and, in such event, the SP hereby indemnifies FP ag ainst any loss, harm or damage which FP may suffer as a result of t he unauthorised disclosure 'of confidential information by a repres entative or affiliate.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.3 The SP shall not disclose the confidential information of FP to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of 'employee, consultant, professional adviser, contractor or sub-contractor or agent of the SP (collectively referred to herein as "representative") of the SP or an affiliate of the SP' and replacing it with 'third party' broadens the scope of non-disclosure. This change creates a contradiction with the exception described in 4.1.3.1, where disclosure to representatives is permissible if necessary for the purposes of 2.1. The current modified text, however, now strictly forbids disclosure to any third party, removing the original agreement of allowing sharing within the SP's own affiliates under specific conditions. Thus this has an effect and creates an in-text contradiction to the 'unless' statement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1.3.1 unless it is strictly necessary for the pur poses referred to in 2.1 above; and
perturbation.justification: The original text in 4.1.3 allowed for the disclosure of confidential information to certain representatives of the SP under specific conditions, as detailed in 4.1.3.1. The changed text broadens the scope of non-disclosure by prohibiting disclosure to 'any third party,' which contradicts the exception previously made for representatives outlined in 4.1.3.1. This creates a direct conflict, as the exception in 4.1.3.1 is no longer compatible with the broader restriction introduced in the modified 4.1.3.

perturbation.original_text: 8.  DOMICILIUM 8.1 The Parties choose as their domiciliae the addresses indicated in the heading to this agreement for the purposes of giving any notic e, the payment of any sum, the serving of any process and for any other purpos e arising from this agreement. 8.2 Each of the Parties shall be entitled from time to time, by written notice to the other, to vary its domicilum to any other address w hich is not a post office box or poste restante. 8.3 Any notice required or permitted to be given in terms of this agreement shall be valid and effective only if in writing. 8.4 Any notice given and any payment made by one pa rty to the other ("the addressee") which – 8.4.1 is delivered by hand during the normal busine ss hours of the addressee at the addressee's domicillum for the tim e being shall be presumed, until the contrary is proved, to have bee n received by the addressee at the time of delivery; 8.4.2 is posted by prepaid registered post from an address within the Republic of South Africa to the addressee at the ad dressee's domicilium for the time being shall be presumed, un til the contrary is proved, to have been received by the addressee on t he fourth day after the date of posting; 8.4.3 is transmitted by facsimile to the addressee' s receiving machine shall be presumed, until the contrary is proved, to have been received within one (1) hour of transmission where it is tra nsmitted during normal business hours or, if transmitted outside no rmal business hours, within one (1) hour of the resumption of nor mal business hours on the next normal business day.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. DOMICILIUM 8.1 The Parties will communicate via email.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the entire domicilium clause and replacing it with a statement about email communication, the contract creates an omission that creates a contradiction. The phrase, creates uncertainty about legal notices and document delivery. While email might be convenient, it lacks the legal standing of a physical address for formal communication. This directly contradicts section 8.3, which requires notices to be in writing, as it's unclear if email satisfies this requirement or holds up in court. It creates confusion, between which form of communication being more relevant.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.3 Any notice required or permitted to be given in terms of this agreement shall be valid and effective only if in writing.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined a domicilium clause including how notices were to be delivered. The changed text replaces this with email communication. Section 8.3 specifies that any notice 'shall be valid and effective only if in writing.' Replacing the domicilium clause with a focus on email creates a direct contradiction. It's now unclear if email constitutes 'in writing' for legal purposes, potentially invalidating notices required under the agreement. This creates a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7.  ENFORCEMENT, GOVERNING LAWS AND JURISDICTION 7.1 This agreement shall be governed by and interpr eted according to the laws of the Republic of South Africa, without reference to the choice of laws' provisions of the Republic of South Africa. 7.2 7.3 The Parties irrevocably submit to the non-exclu sive jurisdiction of the High Court of South Africa, Gauteng Division, in respect of an y action or proceeding arising from this agreement. 7.4 The Parties agree that, in the event of a breac h of this agreement, monetary damages would not be an adequate remedy and that th e plaintiff part shall be entitled to injunctive relief in any court of compe tent jurisdiction and be reimbursed for any costs, claims, demands or liabil ities arising directly or indirectly out of a breach. Nothing contained in th is agreement shall be construed as prohibiting a party or its affiliate from pursui ng any other remedies available to it for a breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. ENFORCEMENT, GOVERNING LAWS AND JURISDICTION 7.1 This agreement shall be governed by and interpreted according to principles of fairness.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific governing law and jurisdiction clause (7.1 and 7.3) and replacing it with a general statement about 'principles of fairness' introduces significant ambiguity. This change directly contradicts the specific clause (7.4) regarding remedies for breach of contract, which assumes a legal framework and court system for enforcement. Without a defined jurisdiction or legal system, the ability to obtain injunctive relief becomes uncertain, as the clause does not specify where or how such relief can be pursued. Therefore, the removal of the original specific location causes a contradiction in the text.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.4 The Parties agree that, in the event of a breac h of this agreement, monetary damages would not be an adequate remedy and that th e plaintiff part shall be entitled to injunctive relief in any court of compe tent jurisdiction and be reimbursed for any costs, claims, demands or liabil ities arising directly or indirectly out of a breach. Nothing contained in th is agreement shall be construed as prohibiting a party or its affiliate from pursui ng any other remedies available to it for a breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies governing laws and jurisdiction, while the changed text replaces this with 'principles of fairness.' This directly contradicts clause 7.4, which assumes a specific legal framework for enforcing remedies like injunctive relief. The change removes the certainty of jurisdiction, making the enforcement of clause 7.4 uncertain and creating a strong in-text contradiction. The removed specific location causes a contradiction in the text.

perturbation.original_text: 2. PREAMBLE The parties hereby declare that: 2.1 The Parties are conducting discussions with a view to concluding one or various agreements relative to the sale of FP to the SP. 2.2 The Parties anticipate that FP will disclose co nfidential information to the SP in the course of their discussions. 2.3 If the confidential information so disclosed is used by the SP for any purpose other than that for which its use is authorised in terms of this agreement or is disclosed or disseminated by the SP to another pers on or entity which is not a party to this agreement, this may cause FP to suffe r damages and material financial loss. 2.4 The Parties have agreed to enter into this conf identiality and non-disclosure agreement, in the absence of which neither party wo uld have disclosed any of its confidential information to the other.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. PREAMBLE The parties hereby declare that: 2.2 The Parties anticipate that FP will disclose co nfidential information to the SP in the course of their discussions. 2.3 If the confidential information so disclosed is used by the SP for any purpose other than that for which its use is authorised in terms of this agreement or is disclosed or disseminated by the SP to another pers on or entity which is not a party to this agreement, this may cause FP to suffe r damages and material financial loss. 2.4 The Parties have agreed to enter into this conf identiality and non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of clause 2.1, which states the core reason for the NDA (discussions about the sale of FP to the SP), creates a contradiction with later clauses defining the permitted use of confidential information. Without 2.1, clause 3, which restricts the use of confidential information to the purpose described in 2.1, becomes vague. The original purpose of confidentiality now is not clear, therefore the original intent is ambiguos, but the rest of the text presumes it exist.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. USE OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION Any confidential information disclosed by a FP shal l be received and used by the SP only for the limited purpose described in 2.1 above and for no o ther purpose.
perturbation.justification: The omission of clause 2.1 removes the defined purpose of the agreement (sale of FP to SP). Clause 3 explicitly states that confidential information shall be used 'only for the limited purpose described in 2.1 above'. Without 2.1, clause 3 becomes ambiguous and contradictory because the 'purpose described in 2.1' no longer exists. This creates a strong in-text contradiction as it renders clause 3 vague and unenforceable, conflicting with its original intent, as described in the provided explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 5. DURATION This agreement shall commence on the date of signat ure of this agreement by the last party to sign the agreement and shall remain in force indefinitel y thereafter (and, in any event, beyond the term of this agreement).
perturbation.changed_text: 5. DURATION This agreement shall commence on the date of signature of this agreement by the last party to sign the agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "and shall remain in force indefinitely thereafter (and, in any event, beyond the term of this agreement)", the contract no longer specifies how long the agreement remains in effect. This omission creates a significant ambiguity. The introduction of the phrase "in any event, beyond the term of this agreement" implies that there is an agreed upon term, however there is no defined term. This leads to a contradiction within this section, due to the removal of the previous end date. The contradiction now indicates that a 'term' is being referenced but not being defined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. DURATION This agreement shall commence on the date of signature of this agreement by the last party to sign the agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the agreement remains in force indefinitely. The changed text removes this specification, implying a potentially finite duration. The explanation states that by removing 'and shall remain in force indefinitely thereafter (and, in any event, beyond the term of this agreement)', the contract no longer specifies how long the agreement remains in effect, thus creating a contradiction within this section, due to the removal of the previous end date. The contradiction now indicates that a 'term' is being referenced but not being defined. The changed text, now lacking a specified duration, contradicts the implied 'term' of the agreement, as highlighted in the explanation.
